main_summary.summary: Lt. Gen. Julius W. Becton Jr., interviewed December 15, 2015 by David Cline (Virginia Tech / Southern Oral History Program) for the Library of Congress Civil Rights History Project, traces a career that spans the Army's transition from Jim Crow segregation to integration. Born in Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania and a 1944 graduate of Lower Merion High School, Becton enlisted in the Army Air Corps hoping to become a Tuskegee Airman, failed the eye exam at Keesler Field, and was redirected to MacDill Field and then to Officer Candidate School at Fort Benning, Georgia in 1944-1945. He served with the segregated 93rd Infantry Division on Morotai alongside the Dixie Division, deployed to Korea with the 9th Infantry Regiment's 3rd Battalion (which included future Congressman Charles B. Rangel), was twice wounded at Pusan Perimeter and Hill 201, and earned a Silver Star. Becton describes Truman's Executive Order 9981 being read at Aberdeen Proving Ground in 1948 with the post commander declaring no change while present; integration came through battlefield military necessity in Korea under Col. C.C. Sloan rather than executive fiat. He commanded at every level through VII Corps in Germany, retired in 1983, then directed USAID disaster assistance during the African famine, ran FEMA under Reagan and Bush, served as DC Public Schools superintendent during the financial control board era, and chaired the Military Leadership Diversity Commission.
key_themes: Military integration via Executive Order 9981 and Korean War necessity
Career trajectory of Black officers through Jim Crow Army
93rd Infantry Division and Korean War combat
Tuskegee Airmen recruitment and the 99th Pursuit Squadron
Multi-generational Black military leadership (Brooks family, Roscoe Robinson Jr., Roscoe Robinson, Arthur Gray)
main_summary.historical_significance:
Becton is one of the small cohort of Black officers commissioned in the closing months of World War II who served continuously through the segregated Army, integration in Korea, Vietnam, and into four-star-adjacent senior command in Germany. His firsthand account of Officer Candidate School integration at Fort Benning (integrated inside the regiment, segregated immediately upon stepping outside it), Aberdeen Proving Ground's post-commander defiance of Truman's executive order, and Col. C.C. Sloan's decision to assign incoming replacements to the 9th Infantry by MOS rather than race documents the operational mechanics of integration at the unit level.

The interview also preserves contemporaneous testimony about the 1965 Selma march and Alabama National Guard deployment from the Army Operations Center, the Military Leadership Diversity Commission's role in opening Ranger School and combat positions to women, and Becton's tenure at FEMA including the John H. Sununu nuclear-plant licensing dispute that ended his federal career. As the highest-ranking Black combat-arms officer of his cohort to publish on the Black officer pipeline (his Vietnam-era branch chief work assigning all armor officers), his account is a primary source on how the Army produced the Roscoe Robinson and Colin Powell generations.

chapters.title: A Janitor's Son on the Main Line
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline of Virginia Tech and the Southern Oral History Program in Washington on December 15, 2015, Lieutenant General Julius Wesley Becton Jr. opens with his birth in Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania, where his father was a janitor and the family lived in a basement a block and a half from Bryn Mawr College, and his schooling at Lower Merion High, whose graduates he reels off from James Billington and Alexander Haig to Kobe Bryant.

chapters.title: In Front of the Mason-Dixon Line
chapters.summary: Becton describes the segregation he grew up under in the Ardmore area in the 1920s and 1930s, schools that were not segregated but restaurants and theaters that were, having to sit in the front rows, the swimming program he could not join, and a high school class he led as president alongside a friend he had known since kindergarten.

chapters.title: Silver Wings and Gold Bars
chapters.summary: He recounts a half-colonel coming to Lower Merion to recruit for the Army Air Corps with the promise of silver wings and gold bars, half the football team going to take the tests, and six passing and being inducted on December 28, 1943, then flunking the flight-school eye test at Keesler Field because of an astigmatism he had hidden by memorizing the standard chart.

chapters.title: A Pickup Truck on the Pavement
chapters.summary: Becton tells of being sent to Keesler Field while his five buddies went elsewhere, a weekend in Biloxi when a pickup truck chased him and a friend onto the sidewalk, and a return decades later as a lieutenant general flown in on his own aircraft, greeted by the Mississippi adjutant general with a state trooper escort, when he told the assembled Guard how things had changed.

chapters.title: I Need a Volunteer Who Can Type
chapters.summary: He describes the segregated barracks and white officers over Black cadre at McDill Field, becoming a first sergeant's clerk on a wet day because he could type, discovering the sergeant could not read and called the roll from memory, and the lieutenant pointing him toward an Officer Candidate School application board.

chapters.title: Integrated to a Point
chapters.topic: Officer Candidate School at Fort Benning
chapters.summary: Becton recounts the OCS board president turning out to be from Lower Merion, going to infantry Officer Candidate School at Fort Benning in January 1945, graduating August 16 just after the atomic bombs, and the way the regiment trained and lived fully integrated while a step outside it returned candidates to the segregated South, separate bus lines, theaters, and service clubs.

chapters.title: Why Put Those Two Together
chapters.summary: Shipping to the Pacific to join the all-Black 93rd Infantry Division, Becton tells of West Pointers on the voyage who urged him never to attend West Point and taught him to shoot craps, landing on Morotai north of New Guinea where the 93rd and the Confederate-flag-flying Dixie Division fought each other when there were no Japanese to fight, riding patrols to keep the peace, and a postwar patrol into the hills where his platoon was shot at by holdouts and ordered not to engage.

chapters.title: My Lieutenant Climbs the Pole
chapters.summary: With the 93rd deactivating in January 1946 and no points to go home, Becton transfers to a Manila signal unit, a heavy construction company stringing overhead cable, where he learns to climb poles from his soldiers and earns their pride by going up a hundred-foot pole himself to hang wire, something the other lieutenants would not do, though he hated heights.

chapters.title: An Excellent Pre-Med Program
chapters.summary: Separated in 1946 but staying in the reserves as a signal officer, Becton describes a call from his old football coach steering him from Lincoln to Muhlenberg College for its pre-med program in February 1947, being the first Black American to go there on a football scholarship, and dropping the scholarship after deliberately ill-fitted equipment gave him a shoulder separation that ended his playing.

chapters.title: My Mother Came on the Honeymoon
chapters.summary: He recalls meeting a young nurse, Louise, and marrying her on January 29, 1948, the day after a final exam in analytic geometry he failed by memorizing formulas without understanding, a professor giving him a B for prior A work, and a honeymoon driving his brother Joe to college in North Carolina with his mother at the wheel.

chapters.title: As Long as I'm Here, No Change
chapters.summary: Becton describes hearing President Truman's Executive Order 9981 to integrate the military read aloud at Aberdeen Proving Ground in 1948, and the post commander immediately declaring that as long as he was there nothing would change, keeping officer club number one and number two, every one white and every two Black.

chapters.title: The Army War College Study
chapters.topic: Why the Brass Doubted Black Troops
chapters.summary: Asked to walk through his feelings, Becton steps back to history: the 369th fighting under the French in World War I and returning jeered rather than greeted, a 1925 Carlisle Barracks Army War College study holding that Black soldiers could do labor but not think through a project or lead, and how that mentality persisted until Benjamin O. Davis Jr. and the 99th Pursuit Squadron began to break it.

chapters.part: On the Road to Fort Bragg
chapters.title: All Deliberate Speed
chapters.summary: Becton explains that the order set no timeline, only all deliberate speed like the later Brown decision, and that a lieutenant would not challenge his post commander, then describes going back on active duty to Fort Bliss as a signal officer applying for infantry and a regular Army commission, and being the junior officer running the mess kitchen on a road convoy from El Paso to Fort Bragg.

chapters.part: On the Road to Fort Bragg
chapters.title: Get Away From the Counter
chapters.summary: He tells of stopping in Van Horn, Texas, where in uniform he was ordered away from a drugstore fountain by a large armed man, leaving without paying, and his white major backing him to the sheriff, then a sedan crashing the next morning and his soldiers, including the sergeant chased off the night before, carrying two injured women to the hospital, the doctors saying the soldiers saved their lives.

chapters.title: A Sergeant Named Rangel
chapters.summary: Becton describes the soldiers' return to Fort Bragg, transferring to the 2nd Division at Fort Lewis on a competitive tour for a regular Army appointment, the 9th Infantry Regiment with one Black battalion and two white, his assignment to the all-Black third battalion of the 9th Infantry, and one of its sergeants, Charles B. Rangel, who would become a congressman.

chapters.title: They'll Break Up the Color Pattern
chapters.summary: He recounts running the regiment's prize drill team, white soldiers asking to join and a lieutenant colonel refusing because they would break up the color pattern, and the unit's hope when the Korean War broke out that the Army would bring back the demanding PhD-holding battalion commander who had trained them rather than the new white commander it installed.

chapters.title: I Didn't Know Where Korea Was
chapters.summary: Becton describes deploying in June 1950 with the 9th Infantry, the first unit to leave the States for Korea, not knowing where Korea was at age twenty, landing at the Pusan Perimeter, his company first ashore, and being sent ahead with a small platoon and communication gear on a separate mission to MacArthur's headquarters to protect an airfield at Pohang.

chapters.title: They Took Us for the Enemy
chapters.summary: He recounts setting up the command post at Pohang while his battalion was ambushed on the same road his patrol had passed safely, the September breakout from the Pusan Perimeter, his platoon leading the company up Hill 201 into heavy fire while the adjacent battalion and the Air Force both mistakenly fired on them, and a messenger weaving marking panels at the planes until the pilots understood, earning a medal.

chapters.title: Home for Christmas
chapters.summary: He recounts being wounded for the first time and recovering at Sendai, Japan, rejoining the regiment as it moved into North Korea near the northern border, leading the battalion again with no winter gear in the cold and mountains, a frozen Thanksgiving turkey, and the Chinese entering the war against MacArthur's promise of home by Christmas.

chapters.title: Are You Satisfied Now, Colonel
chapters.summary: Becton explains being repeatedly picked to lead because he was on a competitive tour and good at it, and recounts being sent up a hill twice on the word of a collaborating adjacent battalion commander, taking a wound the second time, a bullet passing cleanly between the tendon and bone of his left ankle without damaging either, and asking the colonel if he was satisfied.

chapters.title: The Army's First Defeat
chapters.summary: He describes being evacuated by air just as the Chinese and North Koreans chased U.S. forces out of North Korea in what history calls the Army's first defeat, the battalion and regiment taking severe casualties with only five officers surviving, and returning to the same Japanese hospital he had rushed out of before.

chapters.title: Learning to Ski With Two Straps
chapters.topic: Recovery and Command of L Company
chapters.summary: Becton tells of being told to rebuild his strength by learning to ski at Sendai with skis strapped to bare boots, returning to the regiment in March and being put straight into L Company, his old company, which had lost all its officers and needed leadership, over the battalion officer's protest that he had just returned.

chapters.title: Put Them Where They're Needed
chapters.summary: He recounts Colonel Sloan ordering replacements, who arrived listed by military specialty but not by race, to be put where they were needed, which integrated the 9th Infantry from below, his own first non-Black soldier a Mexican American from Texas whom he told his platoon to protect, and Black company commanders soon leading white battalions, integration driven by the need for bodies, not the executive order.

chapters.title: Keep Doing What You've Been Doing
chapters.topic: Two Purple Hearts and a Silver Star
chapters.summary: Becton describes a white captain with no combat experience arriving to command his fully integrated company and telling the first lieutenant to keep running it, finishing the war that way, by which time Becton had earned two Purple Hearts and a Silver Star for the Pusan breakout and Hill 201, before rotating home in May.

chapters.title: Don't You Think I Deserve Better
chapters.topic: From Camp Edwards to Indiantown Gap
chapters.summary: Home and assigned to Camp Edwards to a Black company doing post police and kitchen work for white soldiers, Becton complains to his branch by phone and within an hour is transferred to Indiantown Gap as a basic-training company commander, where he is promoted to captain, integrated into the regular Army, and sent to a signal communications course.

chapters.title: Heavy Foot on the Turnpike
chapters.summary: He fills in the births of his first daughters, Shirley born while he was delayed near Fort Monmouth before Fort Bliss and a second daughter born after the family left Fort Lewis for Korea, and recounts his mother flying in to drive the family, his pregnant company commander's wife, and the dog back to Philadelphia, getting her first speeding ticket on the Pennsylvania Turnpike until he warned that the next one would be her last.

chapters.title: A Mars Station for the Regiment
chapters.summary: He describes joining the 2nd Armored Division in Germany as a captain and communications officer for the 42nd Armored Infantry Battalion, building a military amateur radio (Mars) station no one else had and naming it for Combat Command B, commanding his third company, an armored infantry company with personnel carriers, and the birth of his third child at Fort Benning before Germany.

chapters.title: Don't Talk to My Mother
chapters.summary: Assigned to ROTC duty at Prairie View A&M with no degree yet, Becton recounts a friend in the Department of the Army sending him there to earn his degree, and friends from Germany routing him to a white family's home in Mississippi with the warning to speak only to the father, not the mother, who would not understand a Black officer at her table.

chapters.title: My Mother-in-Law Stayed
chapters.summary: He recalls his mother and mother-in-law visiting them in Germany, his mother-in-law staying with the family until she died and always taking his side in disagreements to Louise's irritation, the third daughter born at Fort Benning, and a Columbus, Georgia officer so impressed by how composedly Louise adjusted, drove a block, and parked that he passed her on the spot.

chapters.title: There's No Way You Can Take a Course
chapters.summary: Becton describes being the only combat-experienced and only regular Army officer among a lieutenant colonel and three majors at Prairie View, his professor of military science blowing up at the idea of his taking classes while teaching, and the colonel relenting the next morning with the warning that any sign it interfered with his instruction meant trouble.

chapters.title: The Five Thousandth Graduate
chapters.summary: He recounts the Army paying his final-semester costs, a new professor of military science bringing in a commencement speaker Becton had met in Washington, and crossing the stage in 1960 to be announced, conveniently for the Army and the university, as Prairie View A&M's five thousandth graduate, a distinction he found about as likely as a snowball in hell.

chapters.title: Sixteen Out of Six Hundred
chapters.summary: Becton describes entering the Army with his diploma and going to the Command and General Staff College at Fort Leavenworth, drawing on relationships built in Germany and at Indiantown Gap, becoming a major there, and being one of sixteen Black officers in a class of six hundred who studied together, all passing except a standoffish lieutenant colonel who failed.

chapters.title: Why Us and Not Germany
chapters.summary: He recounts orders to France out of Leavenworth, six classmates sent to the communications zone rather than the real Army in Germany after a commander there complained of not getting his share of talent, his job in operations as chief of plans preparing the evacuation of families across the border, a fourth daughter born at Prairie View, and Louise earning her degree and serving as acting dean of the college nursing program.

chapters.title: We Don't Have to Carry a Placard
chapters.topic: How the Movement Looked From Inside the Army
chapters.summary: Asked about the wider world during the Montgomery bus boycott and the marches, Becton explains that he and his peers believed they did more for race relations by excelling in the Army than by demonstrating, and recounts his daughter Karen writing anti-war letters to campus papers in South Dakota until he reminded her who was paying her tuition and she stopped.

chapters.title: Floating Around the Army
chapters.topic: Relationships and the Road to General
chapters.summary: Becton reflects on the pride that the military pioneered integration, however bumpily, and how Black officers advanced by being good at their jobs and respected, recounting the lasting relationships from Germany, Leavenworth, and France, including a colonel of his French unit who rose to brigadier general and spoke up for him, that carried him toward his first command at Fort Hood as a brigade commander.

chapters.title: Diversity Is Not Affirmative Action
chapters.summary: Becton describes a returning phone call from a retired colonel now in Army personnel, and his service on a congressional Military Leadership Diversity Commission, drawing the line that affirmative action counts heads while diversity asks what the whole population does to show everyone is qualified, a distinction he says many in and out of uniform still miss.

chapters.title: Women in Ranger School
chapters.summary: He credits the diversity commission with pushing hard to drop the restriction on women in combat jobs, recounts his delight when women entered Ranger School, and tells of his son, a former Old Guard company commander in Washington who resigned his commission because people kept asking after his father, calling during a Rotary dinner with the news.

chapters.title: He Stuck With the Four Stars
chapters.summary: Becton tells of his son preparing the parade ground at the Pentagon when chief of staff Gordon Sullivan and chairman Colin Powell came out to chat, the young captain pressing to get back to work as his exasperated commander glared, and a later dinner where, after his Ranger son phoned that two women had passed Ranger School, a colonel sneered and Becton erupted across the table.

chapters.title: You Better Talk About Marquita
chapters.summary: Becton describes refusing to speak to a fellow resident who slurred the women Rangers until he apologized, his insistence that the Brooks family, who boast of sons promoted to brigadier and four stars, also talk about their lawyer daughter Marquita, Naomi Brooks deciding the family would move in after a luncheon Louise hosted, and Vincent Brooks commanding in the Pacific, whom Becton lent a four-star pin a former boss had once given him.

chapters.part: Joint Schools and a First Black Student
chapters.title: Every Station Played for Our Leader
chapters.topic: France, Kennedy, and the Staff Colleges
chapters.summary: Becton recalls being in France when Kennedy was shot and every French radio station playing music fit for the nation's loss, returning in 1963, attending the Armed Forces Staff College in Norfolk to become a joint staff officer, and his daughter, the only Black child there, becoming the first Black student at a just-integrated school, escorted off the bus by the principal.

chapters.title: Find Out Where the Marchers Are
chapters.topic: The Selma Crisis on the Army Staff
chapters.summary: Assigned in Washington to the deputy chief of staff for personnel handling National Guard and reserve affairs, Becton describes the Selma march and the Army Operations Center, his job to keep communication with the Guard commander on the ground, and pressing a reluctant Guard general by phone for the location of his crew-served weapons by telling him he would report to the chief of staff that the general refused.

chapters.title: McNamara's Whiz Kids
chapters.summary: Becton recounts reuniting in the Pentagon with the West Pointer who once won his money at craps, then being selected with five Army officers for a systems-analysis graduate program at the Institute for Defense Analyses run through the University of Maryland, created to counter the problems McNamara's whiz kids were making for the Army, where he spoke for the military as the senior officer.

chapters.title: The Highest School for Soldiers
chapters.summary: Becton describes a staff group that rewrote what higher-ups disliked, then being selected for the National War College, the third Black Army officer to attend after Roscoe Robinson Jr., the first West Pointer to reach four stars, and a story of Robinson in Hawaii unable to recognize a sweat suit his wife had bleached.

chapters.title: Why Jump Out of an Airplane
chapters.summary: He recounts leaving the War College for the 101st Airborne Division at Fort Campbell to command its only cavalry squadron, getting airborne-qualified at jump school despite hating heights, being the only Black commander at that level, deploying to Vietnam as a squadron and then deputy brigade commander, and Creighton Abrams landing his helicopter to visit the only armor unit in his area, before Becton made brigadier general and went to Fort Dix.

chapters.part: Fort Dix and a Bad Report Card
chapters.title: Don't Close Fort Dix
chapters.summary: As deputy commander at Fort Dix near Philadelphia in charge of training, Becton recounts being sent to Washington to argue against closing the major Northeastern training center, Mayor Frank Rizzo and worried civilians weighing in, the Army telling him to go do his job, and the politicians keeping Fort Dix open to this day.

chapters.part: Fort Dix and a Bad Report Card
chapters.title: Becton Gets the Armored Division
chapters.summary: Becton describes being promoted to major general while a new boss at Fort Dix wanted to be sole commander, learning he would command the 1st Cavalry Division at Fort Hood as it converted to armor despite his thin armor experience, Creighton Abrams's written note directing the assignment, and his boss writing him the worst officer evaluation he ever received, praising his civic skills and panning his training.

chapters.title: One of Two Black Division Commanders
chapters.summary: Taking the 1st Cavalry Division at Fort Hood, Becton notes he and Harry Brooks of the 25th Division in Hawaii were the only two Black division commanders and stayed in touch, praises his three brigade commanders and a chief of staff now a fellow resident, and describes two years of hard field training and new concepts as the family enjoyed the post.

chapters.title: Armored Divisions Don't Ride Horses
chapters.topic: A Change of Command on Horseback
chapters.summary: Becton recounts the corps commander, his predecessor and neighbor, planning a mounted change-of-command ceremony on horseback, Becton's reluctance given his limited riding despite his wife having taught him, and the compromise of his standing and marching on foot, an episode he survived before moving on from the division.

chapters.title: Does It Still Work in a Soldier's Hands
chapters.topic: The Operational Test and Evaluation Agency
chapters.summary: Becton describes moving to the Operational Test and Evaluation Agency at Bailey's Crossroads, the only one who looked like him in the job, a civilian engineer pledging support on condition Becton never overrule his testimony to Congress without warning, and testing the new M1 Abrams tank whose program manager is also a fellow resident.

chapters.title: Held Up Several Months
chapters.summary: Promoted to three stars and given VII Corps, the largest combat corps in the Army with the mission of stopping a Russian push across the border, Becton recounts the announcement being delayed several months while the Army quietly told the outgoing commander and German leaders that a Black American was coming, and his being sent to German language school before the change of command.

chapters.title: You Were Speaking German
chapters.topic: A Change of Command in Two Languages
chapters.summary: Becton tells of preparing his change-of-command remarks alternating English and German, drawing on his Muhlenberg German and his presidency of a German-American club, delivering fifteen minutes of it to the press and assembled soldiers, and the press surprising him by saying they understood he could speak English, after which he never tried to speak German in public again.

chapters.title: He Never Said Sir, Always Boss
chapters.topic: George Patton IV at VII Corps
chapters.summary: Becton recounts his VII Corps deputy George S. Patton IV, the long relationship from Vietnam where Patton resented the attention Abrams gave Becton, through Becton becoming armored branch chief in charge of all armor officers though he had never attended the Fort Knox school, which irritated Patton, who refused to invite a non-armor graduate to address the branch school.

chapters.title: Just Do Your Job
chapters.summary: He describes taking command of VII Corps with Patton as a willing deputy, telling Patton only to do his job, and Patton repeatedly asking to send his family home and transfer back to the States, Becton clearing it with his Heidelberg boss General Blanchard, the same officer who had been his student company commander at Fort Benning.

chapters.title: Hundreds of Tanks on Thawing Ground
chapters.summary: Becton recounts the Reforger exercise moving forces from the U.S. to fall in on prepositioned equipment in Germany, a February thaw leaving hundreds of tanks tearing up German property, his decision to terminate it cleared with his boss, and Alexander Haig flying in as Supreme Allied Commander to make the announcement, the two reminiscing instead about Haig being expelled from their shared high school for smoking.

chapters.title: They Played the Cavalry Song
chapters.summary: Becton describes recovering the corps and German roads after the canceled exercise, Patton finally being sent home over Becton's relayed request to General Blanchard, and the band at Patton's farewell in Stuttgart playing the Garry Owen cavalry song Patton had resented as a division commander, Patton driving off furious as Becton, helpless, almost died, before Becton finished his tour and came home.

chapters.title: The Army's First Inspector for Training
chapters.summary: Becton recounts coming home to the Training and Doctrine Command at Fort Monroe as deputy commander, the only Black American at that level, his old division chief of staff Jack Merritt rising to four stars, and his becoming the Army's first inspector for training, charged not only with the training the Army conducted but with inspecting how well units in the field performed it, which irritated some.

chapters.part: From the Army to the Government
chapters.title: Industry Wasn't Interested in Soldiers
chapters.summary: Becton describes the three-star landscape with Roscoe Robinson going to four stars while he and Art Gray stayed in three-star billets, retiring from the Army in August 1983, and finding that corporations were not interested in what a retired lieutenant general with a master's and ground command could do.

chapters.part: From the Army to the Government
chapters.title: One Look at Me and He Left
chapters.summary: He recounts becoming director of disaster assistance at the Agency for International Development from January 1984 to summer 1985, the famine in Africa his biggest crisis as he coordinated support across agencies, and the South African ambassador, sent to request assistance, taking one look at a Black American in that position and walking out rather than face Becton's view of his country.

chapters.title: A Soldier With No Political Background
chapters.summary: Becton describes contacts made at AID, Vice President Bush and Senator Al Gore among them, speaking on his behalf so he reached FEMA without political support, and reassuring the political appointees who expected anything but a soldier that he was there to do the job and to listen, winning them over so well that three still send Christmas cards.

chapters.title: Sometimes We Said No
chapters.summary: He recounts major floods in Northern Virginia his first week, flying with Senator Warner by Guard helicopter to assess the damage before recommending the president approve a disaster declaration, and explaining that FEMA does not dig ditches itself but coordinates and controls the agencies on the ground, sometimes recommending against a declaration on his experts' advice.

chapters.title: Be My Guest, Governor
chapters.summary: Becton tells of New Hampshire governor John Sununu's fury that FEMA would not certify security for his nuclear power plant until his staff was satisfied, Sununu demanding a change and threatening to go to the White House to no effect, then becoming Bush's chief of staff and vowing to act against him, Bush protecting Becton with an ambassadorship Sununu would not block, and his wife taking the Caribbean posting off the table, ending his relationship with the White House.

chapters.title: I Thought You Said a Board
chapters.summary: Becton describes a call to help the District of Columbia school system, assuming he was to join the control board that ran the schools but learning they wanted him as superintendent, his wife's two conditions that he not bring his anger home and that they not move from Springfield, Virginia, and his insistence to the control board that he would stay in Virginia.

chapters.title: Dad, We're Tired of the Press
chapters.summary: He recounts being the one who went before Congress for school money rather than the jailed mayor, getting along with the mayor until the mayor meddled in his job, and after eighteen months his family holding a caucus, telling him they were tired of seeing his name beaten up in the press, so the practical man reported to the control board and the media that he was going home to be a private citizen.

chapters.title: What Choice Did I Have
chapters.topic: Reflecting on a Life Led First
chapters.summary: Asked to reflect on being so often the first or one of very few, Becton credits help along the way and a supportive family that endured constant moves and modest pay, notes he drew no retirement pay while at AID and FEMA under the federal dual-compensation rule, and recounts joining four corporate boards, beginning with Illinois Tool Works at the urging of former Joint Chiefs chairman Jack Vessey.

chapters.title: He Paid Money
chapters.summary: Becton tells of joining Wackenhut at the call of a Marine friend who noted they had never had a Black board member, the Marine Spill Response Corporation through a retired vice admiral he knew from his testing days, and General Dynamics at the urging of Thomas and Jack Merritt, each board a first for a Black American and each, he jokes, accepted because it paid real money.

chapters.title: In Everybody's Microscope
chapters.summary: Becton reflects that he was the first Black American at each corporation and the first Black corps commander and inspector for training, that the families knew they were under a microscope where every misstep was noticed and reported, and that the most important thing was to demonstrate a Black American could get the job done, asking what choice he had but to do the work.

chapters.title: Maintain a Sense of Humor
chapters.topic: Recognition and a Philosophy of Command
chapters.summary: Becton closes on a Fairfax radio announcement that friends across the country heard, an Army magazine article crediting him with leading the charge at the Pusan Perimeter breakout and Hill 201 though he says he only led his unit, his thirteen-point philosophy of command from his book that singles out maintaining a sense of humor, and his sense of having done all he could for himself, his family, and the country.

loc_video.caption: Julius W. Becton oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Ft. Belvoir, Virginia, 2015 December 15
